subject: general
title: So much paperwork that there's no time left for actual teaching
query_text: So much paperwork that there's no time left for actual teaching

immediate_steps.title: Immediate Action
steps: Identify which paperwork tasks repeat weekly — attendance, lesson plans, registers — and batch them into one fixed 20-minute slot per day, ideally before school starts
Use a single A4 sheet as a weekly 'teaching diary' combining lesson notes + attendance in one place, reducing duplicate writing
Talk to a trusted colleague about sharing register-filling duties or creating a simple class monitor system where students help with routine record-keeping under your supervision

classroom_activity.title: Classroom Activity — Student Helper System
classroom_activity.activity: Assign 4 rotating 'Class Monitors' each week. Each monitor owns one task: attendance marking on the board, distributing notebooks, collecting work, and date/subject writing on the blackboard. You verify in 2 minutes. This reclaims 10–15 minutes of instructional time daily while building student responsibility.

inclusion_strategy.title: Inclusion Strategy
inclusion_strategy.strategy: Give monitor roles first to quieter or struggling students — it builds their confidence and keeps them engaged while you focus on teaching the rest of the class. Rotate roles weekly so no child is excluded.

reflection_prompt.title: Reflection
reflection_prompt.question: After one week of batching paperwork and using student monitors, did you notice even 10 extra minutes of actual teaching time — and how did students respond to the responsibility?

source_citation.title: Source
source_citation.reference: NEP 2020, Section 5.1 — Teacher empowerment and reducing non-teaching burdens; NISHTHA Module — Classroom Management and Student Leadership
